metadata.file_name: 54. Soares. (2024). Combining Statistical and Machine Learning Methods to Identify Predictors of Brazilian Students’ Pro ciency in PISA 2018.pdf
metadata.title: Combining Statistical and Machine Learning Methods to Identify Predictors of Brazilian Students’ Proficiency in PISA 2018

survey_design.weight_fields_interpretation: The study analyzed PISA 2018 public-use microdata for Brazil, which is described as a cross-sectional complex survey with multistage sampling, unequal sampling probabilities, and stratification. The analytic sample comprised 10,691 students from 597 schools, and the authors split the data into two subsamples for the random forest (A1) and multilevel logistic regression (A2). The manuscript does not report applying PISA sampling weights (e.g., W_FSTUWT/TOTWGT) or replicate-weight procedures (e.g., BRR/jackknife) in either the random forest or the multilevel logistic regression steps. Because the models are implemented in Python (sklearn RandomForestClassifier) and the paper does not mention weight-aware estimation, the analysis is treated as unweighted; this may affect population-representativeness and standard error calibration under the complex sampling design.

data.handling_not_reported_explanation: plausible_values_handling is 'not_applicable' — the study likely does not analyze cognitive achievement PVs (e.g., it may focus on affective/attitudinal outcomes, curriculum data, or non-ILSA micro-data).

sample_details.sample_filtering_criteria: Used PISA 2018 public-use data for Brazil, targeting the population of 15-year-old students and their schools. Outcomes were created by dichotomizing PISA proficiency levels into 'Low Proficiency' (below Level 2) vs 'Proficient' (Level 2 or above) separately for mathematics, science, and reading. The authors then created two analytic subsamples for the two-step pipeline: A1 for random forest (n=5345) and A2 for multilevel logistic regression (n=5346). Missingness was handled via multiple imputation by chained equations (MICE) within each subsample using all variables included in the analyses.

confounders_identified.variable_name: ICT as topic in social interaction

main_findings.performance_metrics: Random Forest (A1): out-of-bag accuracy = 87.2% (95% CI: 87.2%–87.3%); top predictors ranked by mean decrease in accuracy (MDA). Multilevel logistic regression (A2): AUC-ROC = 92.5% (95% CI: 91.7%–93.3%).
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2018 Mathematics data for Brazil, the study leveraged annual household income and metacognition subscales (summarizing, assessing credibility, understanding/remembering) along with parents’ highest occupational status to predict mathematics proficiency (Low vs Proficient), finding that higher socioeconomic resources and stronger metacognitive skills increased the odds of being proficient, while grade repetition and additional instruction were associated with lower odds. This indicates that (Low vs Proficient), finding that higher socioeconomic resources and stronger metacognitive skills increased the odds of being proficient, while grade repetition and additional instruction were associated with lower odds.

main_findings.performance_metrics: Random Forest (A1): out-of-bag accuracy = 86.7% (95% CI: 86.6%–86.8%); top predictors ranked by mean decrease in accuracy (MDA). Multilevel logistic regression (A2): AUC-ROC = 91.7% (95% CI: 90.9%–92.6%).
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2018 Science data for Brazil, the study leveraged metacognition (assessing credibility and summarizing) and additional instruction together with annual household income and metacognition understanding/remembering to predict science proficiency (Low vs Proficient), finding that metacognitive skills and socioeconomic resources were positively associated with proficiency, whereas grade repetition and additional instruction were negatively associated with proficiency. main_findings.performance_metrics: Random Forest (A1): out-of-bag accuracy = 86.9% (95% CI: 86.8%–87.0%); top predictors ranked by mean decrease in accuracy (MDA). Multilevel logistic regression (A2): AUC-ROC = 92.8% (95% CI: 92.1%–93.5%).
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2018 Reading data for Brazil, the study leveraged metacognition (assessing credibility and summarizing) and additional instruction along with annual household income and metacognition understanding/remembering to predict reading proficiency (Low vs Proficient), finding that higher household resources and stronger metacognitive skills increased the odds of proficiency, while grade repetition and additional instruction were associated with lower odds. This indicates that ficiency (Low vs Proficient), finding that higher household resources and stronger metacognitive skills increased the odds of proficiency, while grade repetition and additional instruction were associated with lower odds.

data.outcome_summary: The study used PISA 2018 microdata for Brazil (15-year-olds) and dichotomized proficiency into Low vs Proficient (Level 2 or above) for mathematics, science, and reading. A random forest classifier (single-level) identified the most important predictors, achieving out-of-bag accuracy of 87.2% (math), 86.7% (science), and 86.9% (reading). The subsequent adjusted multilevel logistic regression models (school random effects) showed strong discrimination with AUC-ROC of 92.5% (math), 91.7% (science), and 92.8% (reading). Across outcomes, annual household income, parents’ occupational status, early childhood education and care, and multiple metacognition subscales were positively associated with proficiency, while grade repetition and additional instruction were negatively associated. Limitations include cross-sectional design (no temporal causality), self-report predictors, and complex survey weighting not reported; the ML step also used a fixed-effect random forest despite nested data.
